nodes.label: Main question
nodes.title: Is habitual egg consumption net beneficial, harmful, or neutral for human health — overall, for whom, and at what level of intake?

nodes.label: O-1 - 69% of metabolic-ward diet periods compensated for added dietary cholesterol; ~31% showed a plasma-cholesterol rise
nodes.title: Across 75 twelve-week metabolic-ward feeding periods (35% of calories as saturated or polyunsaturated fat, low then high dietary cholesterol), in 69% of the periods subjects compensated for the added dietary cholesterol by lowering fractional cholesterol absorption and/or endogenous cholesterol synthesis, holding plasma cholesterol nearly flat; the remaining ~31% did not adequately suppress synthesis and showed a plasma-cholesterol rise.

nodes.label: O-10 - Daily egg intake associated with lower ischaemic heart disease and major coronary event incidence in China Kadoorie
nodes.title: Daily egg consumption vs never/rarely was associated with lower incidence of ischaemic heart disease (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.84-0.93, P-trend <0.001) and major coronary events (HR 0.86, 95% CI 0.76-0.97, P-trend 0.004) in the China Kadoorie Biobank cohort (30,169 IHD and 5,103 MCE cases).

nodes.label: O-100 - Non-differential dietary measurement error attenuates diet-disease associations toward the null
nodes.title: Under-reporting of diet from food omission is generally non-differential with respect to determinants of the outcome, so it attenuates diet–disease relative risks toward the null (biasing against detection) rather than generating spurious positive associations.

nodes.label: O-101 - FFQ nutrient validity coefficients (~0.4-0.7; energy vs DLW r~0.25-0.32) allow measurement-error correction of estimates
nodes.title: FFQ nutrient validity coefficients are typically ~0.4–0.7 (validity for absolute energy against doubly-labeled water is lower, e.g. r=0.32 in men / 0.25 in women), and such coefficients from biomarker-validation studies can be used to correct diet–disease estimates for attenuation via regression/biomarker calibration.

nodes.label: O-103 - Egg ingestion raised plasma and urine TMAO dose-dependently, with ~11-15% of egg choline converted to TMAO (n=6)
nodes.title: In a double-blind within-subject dose-response (6 volunteers fed breakfast doses of 0, 1, 2, 4, or 6 egg yolks on a controlled low-choline background), egg ingestion raised plasma and 24-h urine TMAO in a dose-dependent manner (P<0.01), with about 11-15% of total dietary choline converted to TMAO (14/14/15/15/11% at 0/1/2/4/6 yolks).

nodes.label: O-104 - TMAO rose significantly only at -=2 yolks and urinary TMAO plateaued between 4 and 6 yolks
nodes.title: The TMAO rise was significant only from 2 yolks upward (1 yolk did not differ from 0; 2 yolks exceeded 0 at P<0.05), and urinary TMAO plateaued at the top of the range (4 and 6 yolks did not differ from each other but both exceeded 0/1/2 yolks), indicating a saturating dose-response.

nodes.label: O-105 - The egg-induced TMAO rise was acute-postprandial (peak 6-8 h), a single-meal flux not a fasting steady-state change
nodes.title: The TMAO rise was an acute postprandial excursion measured over the 24 h after each single egg-yolk meal (peak plasma concentrations at ~6-8 h), i.e. a single-meal flux against a standardized low-choline background, not a measured change in fasting/steady-state TMAO.

nodes.label: O-106 - Marked inter-individual variation in egg-to-TMAO conversion (-=4-fold), all subjects converted, men more than women
nodes.title: There was marked inter-individual variation in TMAO production from eggs (at least a 4-fold difference between subjects); all 6 subjects converted choline to TMAO (no non-converters), and men produced more TMAO than women at equivalent doses.

nodes.label: O-107 - No change in hsCRP or oxidized LDL after egg doses despite the TMAO rise
nodes.title: Despite the dose-dependent TMAO rise, there was no difference in high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP) or oxidized LDL between plasma collected before and 24 h after each egg dose (paired tests, P>0.05).

nodes.label: O-108 - China-PAR U-shaped egg-CVD association lowest at 3 to under 6 eggs per week higher at both extremes
nodes.title: In 102,136 CVD-free Chinese adults (China-PAR; 777,163 person-years, up to 17 y follow-up; 4,848 incident CVD incl. 1,273 CHD and 2,919 stroke), egg intake showed a U-shaped association with incident CVD, lowest at the 3-<6 eggs/week reference: <1 egg/week carried 22% higher risk (HR 1.22, 95% CI 1.11-1.35) and >=10 eggs/week carried 39% higher risk (HR 1.39, 1.28-1.52).

nodes.label: O-109 - China-PAR U-shaped egg-mortality association lowest at 3 to under 6 eggs per week
nodes.title: In the same China-PAR cohort (5,511 deaths), egg intake showed a U-shaped association with all-cause mortality, lowest at 3-<6 eggs/week: <1 egg/week carried 29% higher mortality (HR 1.29, 95% CI 1.18-1.41) and >=10 eggs/week carried 13% higher mortality (HR 1.13, 1.04-1.24).

nodes.label: O-11 - Daily egg intake associated with 26% lower haemorrhagic stroke incidence in China Kadoorie, strongest endpoint
nodes.title: Daily egg consumption vs never/rarely was associated with 26% lower haemorrhagic stroke incidence (HR 0.74, 95% CI 0.67-0.82, P-trend <0.001) - the strongest inverse association among all endpoints - with HR 0.92 (0.90-0.95) per +1 egg/week in the China Kadoorie Biobank cohort (7,078 cases).

nodes.label: O-12 - Daily egg intake associated with 10% lower ischaemic stroke incidence in China Kadoorie
nodes.title: Daily egg consumption vs never/rarely was associated with 10% lower ischaemic stroke incidence (HR 0.90, 95% CI 0.85-0.95, P-trend <0.001) in the China Kadoorie Biobank cohort (27,745 cases).

nodes.label: O-13 - Daily egg intake associated with 18% lower CVD mortality in China Kadoorie
nodes.title: Daily egg consumption vs never/rarely was associated with 18% lower CVD mortality (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.75-0.89, P-trend <0.001) and 28% lower haemorrhagic-stroke mortality (HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.62-0.84, P-trend <0.001), whereas ischaemic-heart-disease and ischaemic-stroke mortality trends were not significant (P-trend 0.131 and 0.718; daily ischaemic-stroke-death HR 1.23, 0.97-1.56).

nodes.label: O-15 - Egg-T2D HR by Harvard cohort - positive in the two women cohorts, null in HPFS men
nodes.title: Per additional egg/day, the fully-adjusted HR for incident T2D was 1.19 (95% CI 1.10-1.29) in NHS (women), 1.15 (95% CI 1.02-1.30) in NHS II (women), and 1.07 (95% CI 0.96-1.18, non-significant) in HPFS (men) - the association was concentrated in the two women's cohorts and null in the men's cohort.

nodes.label: O-17 - Meta-analysis region gradient - egg-T2D RR US 1.18, Europe 0.99, Asia 0.82, p-interaction 0.01
nodes.title: In the 16-cohort meta-analysis the per-egg/day RR for T2D differed by region (p-interaction 0.01): US studies RR 1.18 (95% CI 1.10-1.27), European studies 0.99 (95% CI 0.85-1.15), Asian studies 0.82 (95% CI 0.62-1.09) - positive in the US, null in Europe, null-to-inverse in Asia.

nodes.label: O-18 - Harvard egg-T2D association stronger at lower overall diet quality (AHEI interaction)
nodes.title: In the Harvard cohorts the per-egg/day T2D HR was larger among participants with lower overall diet quality (AHEI) than higher: HR 1.16 (95% CI 1.08-1.25) at low AHEI vs 1.09 (95% CI 0.99-1.20) at high AHEI, p-interaction 0.03.

nodes.label: O-19 - Substituting eggs with whole grains, nuts, yogurt or reduced-fat dairy lowers modelled T2D risk; other animal proteins no change
nodes.title: In modelled isocaloric substitution, replacing 1 egg/day with whole grains, nuts, yogurt or reduced-fat/full-fat dairy or cheese was associated with lower T2D risk (~9-19%), whereas replacing eggs with red/processed meat, poultry, fish, unprocessed meat, legumes, potatoes or refined grains showed no significant change in risk.

nodes.label: O-2 - Plasma-cholesterol rise on added dietary cholesterol coincided with failure to suppress endogenous synthesis
nodes.title: In the periods where plasma cholesterol rose on added dietary cholesterol, that rise coincided with a failure to suppress endogenous (whole-body) cholesterol synthesis.

nodes.label: O-20 - Early stepwise heated-egg introduction cut confirmed egg allergy at 12 months to 8% vs 38% placebo in eczema infants (PETIT RCT)
nodes.title: In a double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT of 121 high-risk (eczema) Japanese infants, oral-food-challenge-confirmed hen's egg allergy at 12 months of age occurred in 8% (5/60) of infants given stepwise heated whole-egg powder from 6 months versus 38% (23/61) given placebo powder; risk ratio 0.221 (95% CI 0.090-0.543), p=0.0001. Both arms received identical aggressive eczema treatment.

nodes.label: O-21 - Early egg introduction raised hospital admissions (10% vs 0%) though acute-reaction rates were similar between arms (PETIT)
nodes.title: In the PETIT RCT, hospital admissions occurred in 6/60 (10%) of the egg-introduction group versus 0/61 in placebo (p=0.022); acute allergic events after trial-powder intake occurred in 9 (15%) egg-group versus 11 (18%) placebo-group participants (19 vs 14 events).

nodes.label: O-25 - 77% of vehicle-identified SE outbreaks in the 1985-87 US Northeast traced to Grade A shell eggs (65 outbreaks, 11 deaths)
nodes.title: From January 1985 to May 1987, 65 foodborne SE outbreaks (2,119 cases, 11 deaths) were reported in the US Northeast; of the 35 outbreaks with an identified food vehicle, 27 (77%) were traced to Grade A shell eggs or egg-containing foods. The implicated eggs were intact, uncracked, and properly refrigerated.

nodes.label: O-26 - SE outbreaks egg-associated 44% of the time vs 15% for other Salmonella serotypes (US, 1973-1984)
nodes.title: National US surveillance data from 1973 to 1984 showed SE outbreaks were associated with egg-containing foods in 44% of cases, versus 15% for outbreaks caused by other Salmonella serotypes.

nodes.label: O-27 - Bulk raw eggs from NE pasteurization plants yielded SE 10% vs 0% elsewhere, tracking human illness geography
nodes.title: Cultures of bulk raw eggs from pasteurization plants in the Northeast yielded S. enteritidis more often (10%) than eggs from other US regions (0%), matching the geographic distribution of human SE illness.

nodes.label: O-28 - Serum cholesterol rises with dietary cholesterol intake in controlled metabolic-ward feeding
nodes.title: In pooled metabolic-ward controlled-feeding experiments in healthy men across dietary-cholesterol intakes spanning roughly 50-1450 mg/day (19 dietary-cholesterol comparisons pooled across 5 institutions, ~22 men per arm at Minnesota), serum total cholesterol increased as dietary cholesterol intake increased, with progressively smaller increments at higher intakes.

nodes.label: O-29 - Cohort of Swedish Men - egg intake and T2D functionally null (p-trend 0.06) with register-based outcomes
nodes.title: In 39,610 Swedish men followed 15y (1998-2012) with register-based (not self-reported) T2D ascertainment (4,173 incident cases), multivariable HRs vs <1 egg/week were 0.98 (95% CI 0.92-1.05) at 1-2/week, 1.11 (95% CI 0.99-1.24) at 3-4/week and 1.11 (95% CI 0.95-1.29) at >=5/week, p-trend 0.06 - a weak, non-significant upward trend, functionally null.

nodes.label: O-3 - Cohort plasma cholesterol more sensitive to dietary fat quality than to cholesterol quantity
nodes.title: Averaged across the metabolic-ward cohort, plasma cholesterol changed more with dietary fat quality (35% of calories as saturated vs polyunsaturated fat) than with dietary cholesterol quantity (low vs high).

nodes.label: O-31 - Wallin meta-analysis US-vs-non-US split - egg-T2D HR 1.18 (US, I2 0%) vs 0.97 (non-US)
nodes.title: In the 12-cohort meta-analysis the per-3-eggs/week HR for T2D was 1.18 (95% CI 1.13-1.24) across 5 US studies with no between-study heterogeneity (I2 0%), versus 0.97 (95% CI 0.90-1.05) across 7 non-US (European + Japanese) studies (I2 78%) - a positive, internally-consistent US signal against a null non-US estimate.

nodes.label: O-32 - Dietary choline or TMAO supplementation increases atherosclerosis in Apoe--- mice, correlating with plasma TMAO and not with plasma lipids
nodes.title: In Apoe-/- mice, ~16 weeks of dietary choline (0.5-1.0%) or TMAO (0.12%) supplementation increased aortic atherosclerotic plaque in both sexes, with plasma TMAO correlating positively with plaque size; plasma cholesterol, triglycerides, glucose, and hepatic lipids did not increase, so the effect was not mediated by standard lipids.

nodes.label: O-33 - Choline, TMAO and betaine upregulate macrophage scavenger receptors CD36-SR-A and promote foam-cell formation in mice
nodes.title: Dietary supplementation with choline, TMAO, or betaine (>3 weeks) increased surface CD36 and SR-A1 scavenger-receptor levels on mouse peritoneal macrophages and markedly enhanced macrophage cholesterol accumulation / foam-cell formation relative to control chow.

nodes.label: O-35 - Antibiotic suppression of gut flora lowers plasma TMAO -100-fold and abolishes choline-induced ~3-fold atherosclerosis and macrophage-CD36 increase in mice
nodes.title: In Apoe-/- mice, a high-choline (1.0%) diet augmented atherosclerosis nearly 3-fold versus control; broad-spectrum antibiotics reduced plasma TMAO more than 100-fold and completely inhibited the choline-induced increases in atherosclerosis, aortic macrophage content, and CD36 expression.

nodes.label: O-36 - Hepatic FMO3 expression and an FMO3-region SNP segregate with atherosclerosis, TMAO, and (inversely) HDL in a mouse F2 cross
nodes.title: In an F2 intercross of atherosclerosis-prone and -resistant Apoe-/- mice, hepatic FMO3 expression (the enzyme oxidizing TMA to TMAO) correlated positively with atherosclerotic lesion area and plasma TMAO and negatively with plasma HDL, and the FMO3-region SNP rs3689151 associated with atherosclerosis (Kruskal-Wallis P<1e-6, dose-dependent by C57BL/6J allele).

nodes.label: O-37 - Plasma choline, TMAO and betaine predict 3-year incident MI, stroke or death in a prospective angiography sub-cohort
nodes.title: In a prospective GeneBank sub-cohort of stable patients undergoing elective coronary evaluation, plasma choline, TMAO, and betaine (identified by untargeted metabolomics) were associated with 3-year incident risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, or death (learning cohort 50 cases vs 50 matched controls; validation cohort 25 vs 25).

nodes.label: O-38 - In 1,876 angiography patients, plasma choline-TMAO-betaine show dose-dependent association with prevalent CVD and TMAO tracks coronary burden
nodes.title: In an independent cohort of 1,876 stable patients undergoing elective cardiac evaluation, elevated plasma choline, TMAO, and betaine showed dose-dependent associations with the presence of cardiovascular disease that held after adjustment for traditional risk factors, and plasma TMAO correlated with angiographic coronary atherosclerotic burden.

nodes.label: O-39 - Replacing one serving per day of eggs with nuts, legumes, whole grains or fish lowers incident CVD by 15-21 percent
nodes.title: In the pooled Lifetime Risk Pooling Project (29,682 US adults; 6,963 incident CVD events; median 19 y follow-up), isocalorically replacing 1 serving/day of eggs with nuts (HR 0.79, 95% CI 0.71-0.88), legumes (0.82, 0.72-0.94), whole grains (0.83, 0.77-0.90) or fish (0.85, 0.77-0.94) was associated with 15-21% lower incident CVD; at 1 serving/week the reductions were 2-3% (30-year absolute risk 0.4-0.7% lower).

nodes.label: O-4 - 3-month high-egg vs low-egg RCT in prediabetes-T2D found no HDL-C difference (mean diff +0.02 mmol-L, P=0.38)
nodes.title: In a 3-month RCT of 140 overweight/obese adults with prediabetes or type 2 diabetes on a weight-maintenance diet, a high-egg diet (2 eggs/day, 6 days/week, ~12 eggs/week) versus a low-egg diet (<2 eggs/week) produced no significant between-group difference in the pre-specified primary outcome, change in HDL cholesterol: mean difference +0.02 mmol/L (95% CI -0.03 to 0.08; P=0.38).

nodes.label: O-40 - Replacing one serving per day of eggs with nuts, fish, poultry or whole grains lowers all-cause mortality by 11-22 percent
nodes.title: Replacing 1 serving/day of eggs with nuts (HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.72-0.86), fish (0.80, 0.73-0.87), poultry (0.81, 0.75-0.89), whole grains (0.83, 0.78-0.89) or legumes (0.89, 0.80-1.00) was associated with 11-22% lower all-cause mortality (8,875 deaths) in the Lifetime Risk Pooling Project; at 1 serving/week 2-3% lower (30-year absolute risk 0.5-1.0% lower).

nodes.label: O-41 - Sign and size of the egg-removal effect depend on the comparator food; eggs rank near processed meat
nodes.title: Within the same model the effect of removing eggs depended on the replacement food: replacing 1 serving/day of eggs with processed meat gave no risk reduction (incident CVD HR 0.99, 0.88-1.12; all-cause mortality 0.93, 0.85-1.02), whereas replacing eggs even with unprocessed red meat still lowered risk (CVD 0.91, 0.83-0.99; mortality 0.89, 0.82-0.96), placing eggs among the least-healthy protein sources, comparable to processed meat.

nodes.label: O-42 - Only 8 percent of participants ate at least one egg serving per day in the pooled US cohorts
nodes.title: In these pooled US cohorts 58.5% of participants consumed >=1 serving/week of eggs but only 8.0% consumed >=1 serving/day; the 15-54% risk reductions reported for 1-serving/day substitutions apply only to the minority whose intake is high enough to be reduced by a full serving.

nodes.label: O-43 - Higher egg intake tracks an unhealthier diet-lifestyle pattern (more red-processed meat, smoking, BMI, T2D) in Harvard cohorts
nodes.title: In the pooled Harvard cohorts, participants with higher whole-egg intake had higher BMI, lower physical activity (NHS/NHS II), more current smoking (HPFS), and more prevalent type 2 diabetes, and consumed more unprocessed red meat, bacon, other processed meats, refined grains, potatoes, full-fat milk, coffee, and sugar-sweetened beverages.

nodes.label: O-44 - Moderate egg intake not associated with CVD in Harvard cohorts (adjusted HR 0.93), crude 1.10 reversing to null on adjustment
nodes.title: In the pooled Harvard cohorts (NHS, NHS II, HPFS; 5,540,314 person-years; 14,806 incident CVD events), the age-adjusted HR for >=1 egg/day vs <1 egg/month was a non-significant 1.10 (0.97-1.23); after full multivariable adjustment for correlated lifestyle and diet (model 3) it was 0.93 (0.82-1.05), with per-1-egg/day HR 0.98 (0.92-1.04), coronary heart disease 0.90 (0.77-1.05), stroke 0.99 (0.81-1.22), and >=2 eggs/day 0.91 (0.72-1.15).

nodes.label: O-45 - Significant egg x type-2-diabetes interaction on CVD in Harvard cohorts, though stratum-specific egg-CVD HRs were null
nodes.title: In the Harvard cohorts a significant interaction was found between egg consumption and prevalent type 2 diabetes on CVD risk (P for interaction <0.001), but egg intake (>=1/day vs <1/month) was not significantly associated with CVD within participants with T2D (HR 1.06, 0.81-1.39) nor within those without T2D (0.93, 0.81-1.06). No interaction was found for age, BMI, physical activity, smoking, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, family history of MI, statin use, or the Alternative Healthy Eating Index.

nodes.label: O-46 - Substituting eggs for red-processed meat or full-fat milk raised CVD risk; substituting for fish-poultry-plant proteins was neutral (Harvard)
nodes.title: In statistical substitution modeling within the Harvard cohorts, replacing 1 whole egg/day with 1 serving/day of processed red meat was associated with higher CVD risk (HR 1.15, 1.05-1.27), unprocessed red meat (1.10, 1.02-1.18), or full-fat milk (1.11, 1.03-1.20); replacing eggs with fish, poultry, legumes, nuts, whole or refined grains, potatoes, reduced-fat milk, cheese, or yogurt was not associated with CVD risk.

nodes.label: O-47 - 33-cohort meta-analysis- no overall egg-CVD association (RR 0.98 per +1 egg-day), but I2=62.3% heterogeneity
nodes.title: An updated random-effects meta-analysis of 33 prospective-cohort risk estimates (1,720,108 participants; 139,195 CVD events) found no association between egg consumption and CVD risk per +1 egg/day (pooled RR 0.98, 0.93-1.03) or for high-versus-low intake (0.99, 0.93-1.06), with substantial between-study heterogeneity (I2=62.3%).

nodes.label: O-48 - Egg-CVD meta-association inverse in Asian cohorts (0.92) but null in US (1.01) and Europe (1.05)
nodes.title: In region-stratified meta-analysis, +1 egg/day was associated with lower CVD risk in Asian cohorts (RR 0.92, 0.85-0.99) but was null in US (1.01, 0.96-1.06) and European (1.05, 0.92-1.19) cohorts; geographic region was identified as the main source of the between-study heterogeneity (P for interaction=0.07; within-region I2: US 30.8%, Asia 44.8%, Europe 64.7%).

nodes.label: O-49 - Meta-analysis in type-2-diabetes populations- higher egg intake associated with higher CVD risk (RR 1.25-1.40)
nodes.title: In meta-analysis restricted to people with type 2 diabetes, higher egg consumption was associated with higher CVD risk (pooled RR per +1 egg/day 1.25, 0.99-1.59; high-versus-low intake 1.40, 1.00-1.97), with considerable between-study heterogeneity.

nodes.label: O-5 - No between-group difference in total cholesterol, LDL-C, or triglycerides in the 3-month egg RCT
nodes.title: Over the 3-month trial in prediabetes/T2D adults, the high-egg diet (~12 eggs/week) produced no significant between-group difference versus the low-egg diet in total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, or triglycerides.

nodes.label: O-50 - Eggs lowered LDL-C less than an energy-matched high-carb breakfast in metabolic-syndrome-prediabetes adults
nodes.title: In 30 completers with metabolic syndrome and/or prediabetes (4-week randomized crossover), LDL-C fell -2.9% on the 2-eggs/day breakfast versus -6.0% on the energy-matched higher-carbohydrate non-egg breakfast (between-diet P=0.023); in absolute terms LDL-C fell ~3.5 mg/dL on eggs despite ~232 mg/day more dietary cholesterol.

nodes.label: O-51 - HDL-C, triglycerides, total-non-HDL cholesterol and hs-CRP did not differ between egg and high-carb breakfasts
nodes.title: In the same 4-week crossover, egg vs non-egg breakfast produced no significant difference in HDL-C (P=0.776), triglycerides (P=0.584), total cholesterol (P=0.353), non-HDL-C (P=0.271), or hs-CRP (P=0.349).

nodes.label: O-52 - HOMA-IR rose far less on the egg breakfast than the high-carb breakfast
nodes.title: In the same 4-week crossover, HOMA-IR (insulin resistance) rose +1.4% on the egg condition versus +24.4% on the non-egg high-carbohydrate condition (between-diet P=0.028) — a glycemic signal favoring eggs.

nodes.label: O-54 - Systolic blood pressure fell more on the egg breakfast than the high-carb breakfast
nodes.title: In the same 4-week crossover, systolic blood pressure fell -2.7% on the egg condition versus 0.0% on the non-egg condition (between-diet P=0.018); diastolic BP did not differ (P=0.351).

nodes.label: O-55 - JPHC (Japan) - no association between egg intake and incident T2D in either men or women
nodes.title: In the Japan Public Health Center-based Prospective Study (27,248 men + 36,218 women aged 45-75, ~5y follow-up, 1,165 self-reported physician-diagnosed incident T2D cases), egg intake showed no association with type 2 diabetes risk in either men or women.

nodes.label: O-57 - The E-value is the minimum RR-scale confounder strength needed to fully explain away an observed association
nodes.title: The E-value is the minimum strength of association, on the risk-ratio scale, that an unmeasured confounder would need with BOTH the exposure and the outcome — beyond the measured covariates — to fully explain away an observed exposure–outcome association (or to move its confidence limit to the null); a large E-value means substantial unmeasured confounding would be required to nullify the estimate, a small E-value means little would.

nodes.label: O-58 - Each +300 mg-day dietary cholesterol associated with higher CVD (HR 1.17) and mortality (1.18) across 6 US cohorts
nodes.title: In 6 pooled US prospective cohorts (29,615 participants; median 17.5-year follow-up; 5,400 incident CVD events, 6,132 deaths), each additional 300 mg/day of dietary cholesterol was associated, in a monotonic dose-response manner, with higher incident CVD (adjusted HR 1.17, 1.09-1.26; absolute risk difference 3.24%, 1.39-5.08) and higher all-cause mortality (adjusted HR 1.18, 1.10-1.26; ARD 4.43%, 2.51-6.36).

nodes.label: O-59 - Each +half-egg-day associated with higher CVD (HR 1.06) and mortality (1.08) before cholesterol adjustment (6 US cohorts)
nodes.title: In the same 6-cohort pool, each additional half-egg/day was associated with higher incident CVD (adjusted HR 1.06, 1.03-1.10; absolute risk difference 1.11%, 0.32-1.89) and higher all-cause mortality (adjusted HR 1.08, 1.04-1.11; ARD 1.93%, 1.10-2.76), in models adjusted for demographic, socioeconomic, and behavioral factors but not for dietary cholesterol.

nodes.label: O-6 - No between-group difference in glycemic control in the 3-month egg RCT in prediabetes-T2D
nodes.title: Over the 3-month trial in prediabetes/T2D adults, the high-egg diet (~12 eggs/week) produced no significant between-group difference versus the low-egg diet in glycemic control.

nodes.label: O-60 - Egg-CVD and egg-mortality associations become non-significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol (6 US cohorts)
nodes.title: After additional adjustment for dietary cholesterol, the egg-CVD association attenuated to non-significance (adjusted HR 0.99, 0.93-1.05; absolute risk difference -0.47%, -1.83 to 0.88) and the egg-mortality association likewise (adjusted HR 1.03, 0.97-1.09; ARD 0.71%, -0.85 to 2.28).

nodes.label: O-62 - Four eggs-day for 4 weeks did not raise fasting TMAO or platelet reactivity in adults with normal renal function
nodes.title: In healthy adults with normal renal function (egg arm n=18), 4 whole eggs/day for 4 weeks did not significantly change fasting plasma TMAO (2.0 -> 2.3 uM, P=0.20), 24-hour urinary TMAO (P=0.28), spot urinary TMAO, or platelet responsiveness (P=0.10).

nodes.label: O-63 - Free choline (choline bitartrate) raised fasting TMAO ~6-fold and increased platelet reactivity
nodes.title: Choline bitartrate tablets (~411 mg free choline/day) raised fasting plasma TMAO roughly 6-fold (1.9 -> 11.1 uM, P<0.0001) and significantly increased 24-hour urinary TMAO (P<0.001) and platelet responsiveness (P<0.01); the effect held across all three choline-tablet-containing arms.

nodes.label: O-64 - Phosphatidylcholine capsules at a matched choline dose did not raise TMAO or platelet reactivity
nodes.title: Phosphatidylcholine capsules (410 mg total choline/day, n=10) did not significantly change fasting plasma TMAO (2.8 -> 3.4 uM, P=0.27), urinary TMAO (P=0.11), or platelet responsiveness (P=0.79) — mirroring the egg-only result.

nodes.label: O-65 - Plasma choline rose significantly in every arm including the egg arm, confirming choline was absorbed
nodes.title: Plasma choline increased significantly in all five arms, including the egg-only arm (P=0.005), with roughly comparable increases across arms — confirming choline was ingested and absorbed even where TMAO did not rise.

nodes.label: O-66 - Platelet responsiveness correlated with TMAO levels across participants
nodes.title: Across participants, platelet responsiveness was strongly correlated with TMAO in both fasting plasma (r=0.51, P<0.0001, N=49) and 24-hour urine (r=0.53, P<0.0001, N=49).

nodes.label: O-67 - Adding egg whites to choline-bitartrate did not suppress the choline-driven TMAO rise
nodes.title: The arm giving egg whites plus choline-bitartrate tablets (n=18) still showed a large fasting TMAO rise (to 28.1 uM, P<0.0001); egg-white antimicrobial peptides had no significant suppressive effect on choline/phosphatidylcholine-dependent TMAO.

nodes.label: O-68 - No lipid-panel component changed in any arm, including 4 eggs-day for 4 weeks
nodes.title: No significant differences were observed in any component of the lipid panel in any arm over the 4-week intervention, including the 4-eggs/day arm.

nodes.label: O-69 - Updated cohort meta-analyses find almost all food groups statistically significantly associated with all-cause mortality
nodes.title: In recent updated meta-analyses of prospective cohort studies, almost all foods showed statistically significant associations with mortality risk — a pattern that in itself is a signature of pervasive bias rather than of ubiquitous causal food effects.

nodes.label: O-7 - High-egg group reported greater postbreakfast satiety and less hunger despite matched protein
nodes.title: In the trial, the high-egg group reported greater postbreakfast satiety and less hunger than the low-egg group, despite matched protein intake between the diets.

nodes.label: O-70 - Taken as lifelong causal, meta-analyzed cohort estimates imply implausible life-expectancy effects including minus 6 years for one egg per day
nodes.title: Assuming the meta-analyzed cohort associations are lifelong causal effects against a baseline life expectancy of 80 years, they imply implausible and mutually incompatible effects: +12 years for 12 hazelnuts/day, +12 years for 3 cups of coffee/day, +5 years for one mandarin orange/day, −6 years for one egg/day, and −10 years for two slices of bacon/day (an effect 'worse than smoking').

nodes.label: O-71 - Serum beta-carotene shows a strong protective mortality association in cohorts (RR 0.69) that randomized trials exclude
nodes.title: Meta-analyzed cohort data give a relative risk of death of 0.69 (95% CI 0.59–0.80) for the highest vs lowest serum/plasma beta-carotene — a strong protective association measured with an objective biochemical assay — yet multiple large randomized trials have excluded even a small mortality benefit (and beta-carotene supplementation showed harm).

nodes.label: O-72 - KoGES (Korea) - egg intake not associated with CVD incidence in the whole cohort
nodes.title: In the KoGES Ansung-Ansan cohort (9,248 Korean adults free of CVD/cancer at baseline, mean 7.3y follow-up, 570 incident CVD cases), egg intake was not associated with CVD incidence in the whole cohort: fully-adjusted HR 1.14 (95% CI 0.87-1.49) for the highest vs lowest intake, p-trend 0.7.

nodes.label: O-73 - KoGES - egg-CVD risk elevated only in the diabetic subgroup (HR 2.81) not in non-diabetics (HR 1.03)
nodes.title: Stratified by diabetes status, highest vs lowest egg intake (~4.2 vs ~0.1 servings/week) was associated with CVD among the 615 participants WITH T2DM (HR 2.81, 95% CI 1.25-6.30, p-trend 0.02; 79 CVD cases) but not among the 8,633 WITHOUT T2DM (HR 1.03, 95% CI 0.77-1.38, p-trend 0.8) - a large adverse signal confined to the pre-existing-diabetes subgroup.

nodes.label: O-74 - KoGES - egg intake not associated with fasting blood lipids across quartiles
nodes.title: Across egg-intake quartiles in the KoGES cohort there were no significant differences in fasting total cholesterol (4.80-4.86 mmol/L, p-trend 0.2), HDL-C (1.22-1.23, p-trend 0.3), LDL-C (2.84-2.88, p-trend 0.3) or triglycerides (1.55-1.65, p-trend 0.3).

nodes.label: O-75 - Fasting total cholesterol rose 1.47 mg-dL per 100 mg dietary cholesterol across a 0-4 egg-day crossover (linear, no threshold)
nodes.title: In 20 healthy young men in an 8-week four-way randomized crossover feeding 0, 1, 2, or 4 eggs/day (128-858 mg dietary cholesterol/day), fasting plasma total cholesterol increased by 1.47 mg/dL (0.038 mmol/L) for every 100 mg dietary cholesterol added, linearly across the full range with no apparent threshold (P<0.001).

nodes.label: O-76 - Fasting LDL cholesterol rose 1.38 mg-dL per 100 mg dietary cholesterol (P-0.001)
nodes.title: Fasting LDL cholesterol increased by 1.38 mg/dL for each additional 100 mg/day of dietary cholesterol across the 0-4 egg range (P<0.001), i.e. nearly all of the total-cholesterol rise was carried by LDL.

nodes.label: O-77 - No significant dose response of HDL cholesterol or triglycerides to dietary cholesterol
nodes.title: Across the four egg/cholesterol doses there was no significant dose response for HDL cholesterol or for triglycerides; the dietary-cholesterol effect fell essentially entirely on LDL/total cholesterol, not HDL.

nodes.label: O-78 - Fasting apolipoprotein B rose ~10% between 0- and 4-egg diets, correlated with total-LDL cholesterol changes
nodes.title: Fasting plasma apolipoprotein B increased by approximately 10% between the 0-egg and 4-egg diets and was correlated with the changes in total and LDL cholesterol.

nodes.label: O-8 - China Kadoorie egg intake calibrated to 0.29-0.76 eggs per day across categories
nodes.title: Egg intake in the China Kadoorie Biobank was assessed by a single-baseline non-validated qualitative FFQ whose top category was 'daily' (no >1 egg/day option); calibration against a 2013-14 resurvey put mean intake at 0.29 egg/day (never/rarely), 0.36 (1-3 days/month), 0.46 (1-3 days/week), 0.56 (4-6 days/week) and 0.76 egg/day (daily), so the studied exposure range lies entirely below ~1 egg/day.

nodes.label: O-80 - Individual cholesterol responsiveness varied widely, appeared normally distributed, and was mostly positive; ApoE4 only a non-significant trend
nodes.title: Individual responsiveness of plasma cholesterol to dietary cholesterol varied widely, appeared normally (continuously) distributed with mostly positive responses, and showed only a non-significant trend toward larger responses in apolipoprotein E4 carriers.

nodes.label: O-81 - APOE genotype did not modify the LDL-C response to dietary cholesterol (or trans-fat) in a pooled n=395 feeding dataset
nodes.title: In a pooled reanalysis of 395 mostly normolipidemic subjects from earlier controlled-feeding trials with retrospective APOE genotyping, the LDL-C/serum-cholesterol response to a dietary-cholesterol challenge did not differ significantly between APOE genotypes (E3/3 vs E3/4 or E4/4); the response to trans-fat likewise did not differ by genotype.

nodes.label: O-82 - LDL-C response to saturated fat was slightly larger in APOE E3-4 or E4-4 carriers than E3-3
nodes.title: The LDL-C response to saturated fat was 0.08 mmol/L larger in APOE3/4 or E4/4 carriers than in E3/3 carriers (95% CI -0.01 to 0.18 mmol/L) — a small effect whose confidence interval crosses zero.

nodes.label: O-84 - PURE median egg intake 3.9 per week ranging to about 1.2 eggs per day with regional variation
nodes.title: In PURE the median egg intake was 3.9 eggs/week (highest category median 8.6/week, ~1.2 eggs/day; reference <1/week), varying regionally from 5.6/week in China to 1.4/week in South Asia; the study's exposure contrast (>=7 vs <1 egg/week) thus spans roughly none to ~1 egg/day.

nodes.label: O-85 - Higher egg intake not associated with adverse blood lipids and associated with slightly lower blood pressure in PURE
nodes.title: In PURE (n=114,615 with lipids), >=7 vs <1 egg/week showed no association with total cholesterol (4.89 vs 4.90 mmol/L), LDL-C (3.08 vs 3.07), HDL-C (1.20 vs 1.21), TC:HDL ratio (4.30 vs 4.27) or ApoB:ApoA1 (all P-trend >0.20), while higher intake was associated with lower systolic and diastolic blood pressure (both P-trend <0.0001); dietary cholesterol was likewise unassociated with blood lipids.

nodes.label: O-86 - In PURE one or more eggs per day not associated with composite CVD-mortality, total mortality, or major CVD
nodes.title: Among 146,011 PURE adults (median 9.5 y follow-up; 14,700 composite events) without prior CVD, >=7 vs <1 egg/week showed no significant association with the composite of death or major CVD (HR 0.96, 95% CI 0.89-1.04, P-trend 0.74), total mortality (1.04, 0.94-1.15, P-trend 0.38), major CVD (0.92, 0.83-1.01, P-trend 0.20), cardiovascular mortality (1.00, 0.85-1.19), stroke (0.97, 0.84-1.13) or heart failure (1.24, 0.90-1.70; ns).

nodes.label: O-87 - Higher egg intake associated with lower myocardial infarction risk in PURE but not replicated in ONTARGET TRANSCEND
nodes.title: In PURE, >=7 vs <1 egg/week was associated with lower myocardial infarction risk (HR 0.84, 95% CI 0.72-0.98, P-trend 0.02 in CVD-free participants) - the only nominally significant clinical association - but this was absent in ONTARGET/TRANSCEND (MI HR 1.12, 0.68-1.82, ns), and the authors flag it as a likely multiple-testing artefact.

nodes.label: O-88 - PURE egg-CVD association appeared protective only in participants with prevalent CVD, interaction non-significant
nodes.title: In PURE, higher egg intake was associated with lower composite risk among participants with prevalent CVD at baseline (>=7/wk HR 0.81, 0.70-0.95) but not among those without (P-interaction 0.24); the apparent protective subgroup association was not reproduced in the two secondary-prevention cohorts (ONTARGET/TRANSCEND).

nodes.label: O-89 - In 31544 vascular-disease patients ONTARGET TRANSCEND egg intake not associated with CVD or mortality
nodes.title: In 31,544 patients with vascular disease or diabetes with end-organ damage (ONTARGET/TRANSCEND, median 4.7 y follow-up), >=7 vs <1 egg/week showed no significant association with the composite outcome (HR 0.97, 0.76-1.25), total mortality (0.88, 0.62-1.24), major CVD (0.97, 0.73-1.29) or MI (1.12, 0.68-1.82); a positive trend for heart failure was noted (P-trend 0.01, top-category HR 1.25, 0.74-2.11).

nodes.label: O-9 - Daily egg intake associated with 11% lower total CVD incidence in China Kadoorie Biobank
nodes.title: Among 461,213 CVD-free Chinese adults (83,977 incident CVD cases, median 8.9 y follow-up), daily egg consumption vs never/rarely was associated with lower total CVD incidence (HR 0.89, 95% CI 0.87-0.92), with a graded monotonic dose-response across categories (1-3/month 0.97, 1-3/week 0.92, 4-6/week 0.90, daily 0.89; P-trend <0.001; HR 0.97 per +1 egg/week).

nodes.label: O-91 - In OPEN (n=484) the FFQ under-reported energy by ~31-38% and the 24-hour recall by ~12-20%, FFQ roughly twice as biased
nodes.title: In the OPEN study (484 adults aged 40–69), reported energy intake versus doubly-labeled water was under-reported by ~31–36% (men) / 34–38% (women) on the FFQ and by ~12–14% (men) / 16–20% (women) on the 24-hour recall — i.e. the FFQ's energy under-reporting was roughly double that of the 24-hour recall, and larger in women.

nodes.label: O-92 - OPEN protein under-reporting was ~27-34% on FFQ and ~11-15% on 24HR, but protein density was barely biased
nodes.title: In OPEN, reported protein intake versus the urinary-nitrogen biomarker was under-reported by ~30–34% (men) / 27–32% (women) on the FFQ and ~11–12% (men) / 11–15% (women) on the 24-hour recall, whereas the percentage of energy from protein (protein density, a ratio measure) showed little under-reporting for either sex.

nodes.label: O-93 - In OPEN the magnitude of dietary under-reporting increased with body mass index (person-specific systematic bias)
nodes.title: In OPEN, the magnitude of energy and protein under-reporting increased with body mass index — higher-BMI participants under-reported more — showing that the self-report error contains a systematic, person-specific component correlated with a participant characteristic rather than being purely random noise.

nodes.label: O-94 - Choline-free diet for 3 weeks depleted plasma choline and phosphatidylcholine ~30% in healthy men
nodes.title: In healthy male volunteers fed a semisynthetic choline-devoid diet (adequate but not excess methionine) for 3 weeks, plasma choline and phosphatidylcholine fell ~30% and plasma/erythrocyte phosphatidylcholine ~15%; no such change occurred in the choline-continued control group.

nodes.label: O-95 - Choline deficiency raised serum ALT (0.42 to 0.62 mukat-L) in deficient men only, other liver-renal tests unchanged
nodes.title: During the 3-week choline-deficient period, serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) rose steadily from a mean 0.42 to 0.62 mukat/L in the deficient group only (a sign of incipient liver dysfunction), while other liver and renal function tests were unchanged and the control group showed no change; the biochemical signs reversed on repletion.

nodes.label: O-96 - Choline deficiency lowered serum cholesterol ~15% in deficient men only
nodes.title: Serum cholesterol decreased an average of 15% in the choline-deficient group over the 3-week deficient period, with no change in the choline-continued control group.

nodes.label: O-97 - 3 whole eggs-day raised HDL-C in overweight men on a carbohydrate-restricted diet, while egg substitute did not
nodes.title: In 28 overweight/obese men on a 12-week carbohydrate-restricted diet, the group eating 3 whole eggs/day (640 mg/day added dietary cholesterol) increased HDL-C from 1.23 +/- 0.39 to 1.47 +/- 0.38 mmol/L (P<0.01), whereas the energy-matched egg-substitute (0 cholesterol) group showed no change in HDL-C.

nodes.label: O-98 - LDL-C and the LDL-HDL ratio did not change with egg intake, including in dietary-cholesterol hyper-absorbers
nodes.title: Adding 3 whole eggs/day did not change LDL-C or the LDL-C:HDL-C ratio during the intervention, and the LDL:HDL ratio was maintained similarly in both hyper-absorbers and hypo-absorbers of dietary cholesterol.

nodes.label: O-102 - Small relative risks carry large absolute burden- trans fat's +29% IHD risk implies 6,480-12,960 US IHD deaths per year
nodes.title: A modest-sounding relative risk can carry large absolute public-health impact: replacing 2% of energy from carbohydrate with trans fat is associated with a 29% (95% CI 11–49%) increase in ischemic-heart-disease risk, corresponding to an estimated 6,480–12,960 preventable US IHD deaths annually.

nodes.label: O-14 - Pooled Harvard 3-cohort egg-T2D HR 1.14 per additional egg per day
nodes.title: In 213,798 US health professionals (NHS 1980-2012, NHS II 1991-2017, HPFS 1986-2016; 5,529,959 person-years; 20,514 incident type 2 diabetes cases), each additional egg/day carried a fully-adjusted pooled HR for incident T2D of 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.20); the categorical contrast >=1 egg/day vs <1 egg/month gave HR 1.23 (95% CI 1.09-1.37).

nodes.label: O-16 - Updated 16-cohort meta-analysis - overall egg-T2D RR 1.07 per egg per day, non-significant
nodes.title: An updated dose-response meta-analysis of 16 prospective cohorts (589,559 participants, 41,248 incident T2D cases) gave a pooled overall RR of 1.07 (95% CI 0.99-1.15) per 1 egg/day, non-significant, with substantial between-study heterogeneity (I2 ~70%).

nodes.label: O-22 - Confirmed egg-allergy prevalence 1.6% at age 2.5 years in a Norwegian birth cohort (cumulative incidence ~2.6%)
nodes.title: In a Norwegian population-based birth cohort (n=2,721), the point prevalence of objectively-confirmed egg allergy at mean age 2.5 years was 1.6% (95% CI 1.3-2.0%), with an estimated upper-bound cumulative incidence by that age of ~2.6% (95% CI 1.6-3.6%). Almost all confirmed reactions were IgE-mediated.

nodes.label: O-23 - Parent-reported egg reactions confirmed ~two-thirds of the time; PPV rises from 50% to 100% with repeated reporting
nodes.title: In the cohort, roughly two-thirds of parent-reported egg reactions were confirmed by objective testing; the positive predictive value of a parent-reported reaction rose from ~50% (reported once) to 100% (reported three times), and objectively-present but unreported (unrecognized) reactions were infrequent — so parent report alone substantially over-estimates true egg-allergy prevalence.

nodes.label: O-24 - Reported Salmonella enteritidis infections rose -6-fold in the northeastern US, 1976-1986
nodes.title: Reported Salmonella enteritidis (SE) infections in the northeastern United States rose more than 6-fold from 1976 to 1986, an epidemic increase concentrated in that region.

nodes.label: O-30 - Wallin 12-cohort meta-analysis - overall egg-T2D HR 1.03 per 3 servings per week, non-significant, no nonlinearity
nodes.title: An updated dose-response meta-analysis of 12 prospective cohorts (287,963 participants, 16,264 T2D cases) gave a pooled overall HR of 1.03 (95% CI 0.96-1.10) per 3 eggs/week, non-significant, with high heterogeneity (I2 78%); no evidence of a non-linear dose-response in any study (p-nonlinearity >=0.15 for all).

nodes.label: O-34 - Gut flora are obligately required to convert dietary phosphatidylcholine-choline to TMAO in mice (germ-free and antibiotic abolition, conventionalization restores)
nodes.title: In mice, germ-free status and 3-week broad-spectrum antibiotic pretreatment completely abolished appearance of d9-TMAO after oral d9-phosphatidylcholine or d9-choline; re-colonizing antibiotic-treated mice with normal flora ('conventionalization') restored d9-TMAO production.

nodes.label: O-53 - The primary outcome, IVGTT-derived whole-body insulin sensitivity index, did not differ between egg and high-carb breakfasts
nodes.title: The trial's pre-specified primary outcome — the IVGTT-derived insulin sensitivity index (ISI) — did not differ significantly between conditions (egg +15.0% vs non-egg -4.1%, P=0.193), directionally favoring eggs but non-significant; HOMA2-%S (P=0.067) and fasting insulin (P=0.057) were likewise non-significant.

nodes.label: O-56 - JPHC - dietary cholesterol not associated with T2D in men; inverse in postmenopausal women (OR 0.68)
nodes.title: In the same JPHC cohort, dietary cholesterol intake was not associated with T2D in men; in women it trended inverse (highest vs lowest quartile ~23% lower, non-significant overall) and reached significance in postmenopausal women (multivariable OR 0.68, 95% CI 0.49-0.94, p-trend 0.04).

nodes.label: O-61 - Eggs are a major dietary source of cholesterol (~185-200 mg per large egg)
nodes.title: Eggs are a major dietary source of cholesterol -- one large egg contains roughly 185-200 mg of cholesterol, concentrated in the yolk -- making egg intake and total dietary-cholesterol intake strongly correlated in typical diets.

nodes.label: O-79 - No difference in postprandial lipid responses across dietary-cholesterol levels
nodes.title: There were no differences in the postlunch or post-fat-formula (postprandial) plasma-lipid responses across the four diets, although absolute baseline total and LDL cholesterol stayed elevated on the higher-cholesterol diets throughout the postprandial period.

nodes.label: O-83 - LDL-C response to cafestol was smaller in APOE E4 carriers (opposite-direction genotype effect)
nodes.title: The LDL-C response to the coffee diterpene cafestol was 0.11 mmol/L smaller in APOE3/4 or E4/4 carriers than in E3/3 carriers (95% CI -0.29 to 0.07 mmol/L) — an opposite-direction genotype effect to that seen for saturated fat, and again with a CI crossing zero.

nodes.label: O-90 - Self-reported dietary intake systematically under-reports true energy intake relative to the doubly-labeled-water biomarker
nodes.title: Self-reported energy intake — from both food-frequency questionnaires and 24-hour recalls — is systematically under-reported relative to total energy expenditure measured by the doubly-labeled-water recovery biomarker in weight-stable adults; this systematic downward bias is an established, replicated property of memory-based dietary self-report.

nodes.label: O-99 - On the carbohydrate-restricted diet, triglycerides, body weight, and metabolic-syndrome prevalence all fell across both groups
nodes.title: Across all subjects on the 12-week carbohydrate-restricted diet (both egg and substitute groups), plasma triglycerides fell from 1.34 +/- 0.66 to 0.83 +/- 0.30 mmol/L (P<0.001), body weight decreased (P<0.0001), and the number of subjects meeting metabolic-syndrome criteria fell from 18 to 3.
